teaching_arc.entry_point: Start by treating the robot as a system rather than just an arm.
teaching_arc.progression: Move through the main components, then connect each one to its practical role in the cell.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should leave with a whole-system mental model they can reuse in later control, sensing, and programming topics.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Help students see an industrial robot as a coordinated system of mechanical, electrical, sensing, and control components.
topic_takeaways: Students should identify the main subsystems inside a robot cell.
Students should understand how controllers, actuators, sensors, and end effectors interact.
Students should be able to explain how component choices affect robot behavior in practice.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Keep linking parts back to their practical function.
Use visuals to show how energy, signals, and motion move through the system.
transition_style: Move from the industrial question students would ask on the job to the next specification, control, or programming choice they need to make.

slides.narration_seed: Hi everyone. Today we’ll break a robot into its main building blocks: the mechanical structure, joints and links, end effectors, sensors, actuators, and the controller.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Help students see an industrial robot as a coordinated system of mechanical, electrical, sensing, and control components.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Why do we learn components? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Robot Components as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Robot Components connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Robot Components sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: When something fails on a robot, the fastest way to debug is to ask: - Is the command correct? - Did the controller output the signal?
must_cover: Troubleshooting = knowing the blocks and the connections between them
Most robot problems show up as: sensing issues, actuation issues, wiring/I/O issues, or software logic mistakes
If you can map the chain (command → motion → feedback), you can find where it breaks
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Robot Components into Why do we learn components? so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: The next slide should compress the lesson into a quick memory or self-check.
slides.explanation_style: Use Why do we learn components? to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Why do we learn components? connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Why do we learn components? sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slide_ref.source_title: The robot as a system (the one picture to remember)

slides.narration_seed: This is the most important slide today. Everything we discuss fits into this loop: command → compute → control → motion → measurement → correction.
must_cover: Task logic (software) defines what the robot should do (task/sequence)
Motion computation (processor) computes how to move (paths/joint targets)
Feedback control (controller) continuously reduces error to hit the target
Students should identify the main subsystems inside a robot cell.
Actuators create joint motion (motors, valves, cylinders)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use The robot as a system (the one picture to remember) to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Robot body types (most common) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use The robot as a system (the one picture to remember) as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep The robot as a system (the one picture to remember) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave The robot as a system (the one picture to remember) with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

slides.narration_seed: When people say 'robot' in factories, they usually mean a manipulator. In warehouses and hospitals, you’ll often see mobile robots.
must_cover: Manipulator = robot arm for positioning/orientation of a tool
Mobile robot = AMR/rover for moving in the environment
Mobile manipulator = mobile base + arm (move + manipulate)
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Links and joints (simple) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
prop_suggestions: Mobile robot example (laser scanner + camera + gripper).
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Robot body types (most common) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Robot body types (most common) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Robot body types (most common) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Think of the robot as a chain. Links are the solid pieces.
must_cover: Links = rigid bodies that carry loads (structure of the robot)
Joints = where motion happens (rotation or translation)
Robot arm = a chain of links connected by joints
DOF (degrees of freedom) = number of independent joint motions you can command
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Joint type #1: Revolute (R) — rotates like a hinge so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Links and joints (simple) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Links and joints (simple) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Links and joints (simple) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: Joint type #1: Revolute (R) — rotates like a hinge

slides.narration_seed: In real industrial arms, revolute joints are the default because they’re compact and give a big workspace.
must_cover: Rotation around one axis (like a door hinge)
Most common joint in industrial robot arms (compact and practical)
Good when you need a large reachable workspace around the robot
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Joint type #2: Prismatic (P) — slides like a piston so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Joint type #1: Revolute (R) — rotates like a hinge first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Joint type #1: Revolute (R) — rotates like a hinge connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Joint type #1: Revolute (R) — rotates like a hinge clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: Joint type #2: Prismatic (P) — slides like a piston

slides.narration_seed: Prismatic joints show up a lot in Cartesian robots and vertical Z motion. If you need precise box-shaped movement, prismatic axes are perfect.
must_cover: Translation along one axis (straight-line motion)
Very common in Cartesian/gantry robots (X–Y–Z stages)
Great when you want box-shaped, highly repeatable motion
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quick quiz #1 (joints) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Joint type #2: Prismatic (P) — slides like a piston first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Joint type #2: Prismatic (P) — slides like a piston connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Joint type #2: Prismatic (P) — slides like a piston clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Correct answer is B. In industry, a typical 6-axis robot is essentially six revolute joints: base, shoulder, elbow, and a 3-axis wrist.
must_cover: Which joint type is MOST common in a 6-axis industrial arm?
Most 6-axis industrial arms are basically 6 revolute joints. Prismatic joints are more common in Cartesian/gantry systems.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quick quiz #1 (joints) and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quick quiz #1 (joints) into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quick quiz #1 (joints) question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slide_ref.source_title: Other joint types (less common, but good to know)

slides.narration_seed: You won’t always use these names in industry, but you will see these motions in mechanisms. Revolute and prismatic are still the main ones in most robot arms.
must_cover: Cylindrical (C) = rotate + slide on the same axis
Spherical (S) = 3 rotations at one point (ball-joint idea)
Planar = motion in a plane (x–y) + rotation about z
Helical = rotation + translation are linked (like a screw thread)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Other joint types (less common, but good to know) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Other joint types (less common, but good to know) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Define Other joint types (less common, but good to know) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Other joint types (less common, but good to know) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Other joint types (less common, but good to know) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: If you’re moving in a box-like area, a Cartesian robot makes sense. If you need to reach around objects, arms are better.
must_cover: Workspace = the set of all positions/orientations the robot can reach
Revolute joints → larger, round workspace (reach around obstacles)
Prismatic joints → box-shaped workspace (great for rectangular areas)
Robot choice depends on task geometry: where do I need to reach?
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Workspace (why joint choices matter) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Workspace (why joint choices matter) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Define Workspace (why joint choices matter) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Workspace (why joint choices matter) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Use a simplified robot silhouette and workspace volume so students can see how geometry shapes where the robot can work.

slides.narration_seed: The robot arm is like the ‘body’—but the end effector is what touches the work. Many applications succeed or fail because of EOAT design.
must_cover: EOAT = End Of Arm Tooling (the part that touches the work)
Same robot arm + different EOAT = completely different application
In industry, EOAT design often decides speed, reliability, and quality
slides.transition_from_previous: Use End effector (EOAT) = the robot’s tool to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once End effector (EOAT) = the robot’s tool is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define End effector (EOAT) = the robot’s tool in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep End effector (EOAT) = the robot’s tool connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain End effector (EOAT) = the robot’s tool accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slides.narration_seed: If I had to pick the most common mechanical gripper: parallel-jaw. Simple open/close, and it works for many tasks.
must_cover: Strong, simple, reliable open/close gripping
Best for rigid parts with predictable geometry (edges/flats)
Common in labs because it’s versatile and easy to control
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Gripper #2: Vacuum (super common in packaging) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Gripper #1: Parallel-jaw (most common) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Gripper #1: Parallel-jaw (most common) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Gripper #1: Parallel-jaw (most common) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slides.narration_seed: Vacuum is extremely common in pick-and-place. But if the surface is rough, porous, or oily—vacuum can fail.
must_cover: Fast pick-and-place on flat surfaces (boxes, sheets, bags)
Very common on conveyors and in packaging automation
Limitations: porous/rough surfaces, leaks, oily/dusty parts
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Gripper #3: Magnetic (for steel parts) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Gripper #2: Vacuum (super common in packaging) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Gripper #2: Vacuum (super common in packaging) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Gripper #2: Vacuum (super common in packaging) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slides.narration_seed: Magnetic gripping is excellent for steel handling. But we must think about safety—power loss and drop risk.
must_cover: Best for steel sheets and ferromagnetic parts (fast handling)
Very fast grip/release and simple mechanical design
Safety note: power loss can cause drop risk (use fail-safe designs)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Gripper #3: Magnetic (for steel parts) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Gripper #3: Magnetic (for steel parts) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Gripper #3: Magnetic (for steel parts) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Gripper #3: Magnetic (for steel parts) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Gripper #3: Magnetic (for steel parts) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slides.narration_seed: Multi-finger hands look impressive, but in industry they’re less common than parallel or vacuum. Industry usually chooses reliability and speed.
must_cover: More adaptable grasping for irregular objects
Useful for research and special handling tasks
Downside: cost + complexity + often slower and less robust than simple grippers
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Tool end effectors (very common in industry) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Gripper #4: Multi-finger (dexterous, but complex) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Gripper #4: Multi-finger (dexterous, but complex) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Gripper #4: Multi-finger (dexterous, but complex) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slide_ref.source_title: Tool end effectors (very common in industry)

slides.narration_seed: Many industrial robots don’t ‘grab’ parts—they perform a process. Welding and painting are classic examples because they are repetitive and hazardous for humans.
must_cover: Welding torch (arc/spot welding) → consistent weld quality and speed
Paint gun (spray painting) → uniform coating and safer working conditions
Screwdriver (assembly) → repeatable torque and fast cycle times
Dispensing (glue / sealant) → controlled bead width and placement
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Tool end effectors (very common in industry) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Tool end effectors (very common in industry) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Tool end effectors (very common in industry) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Tool end effectors (very common in industry) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Tool end effectors (very common in industry) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slides.narration_seed: A strong arm won’t help if the gripper slips or damages parts. In real projects, EOAT is often the hardest practical engineering.
must_cover: Pick EOAT based on: object + process + environment (dust, oil, heat, cleanliness)
Ask: Do we need force control? high speed? high reliability? no contamination?
Common failure: great robot arm + wrong EOAT → slipping, dropping, or damaging parts
slides.transition_from_previous: Use EOAT selection (simple rule) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once EOAT selection (simple rule) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define EOAT selection (simple rule) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep EOAT selection (simple rule) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain EOAT selection (simple rule) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slides.narration_seed: Correct answer is A. Vacuum is the industry default for boxes and flat objects (as long as the surface allows a seal).
must_cover: You must pick 500 flat cardboard boxes from a conveyor—fast. Best EOAT?
Vacuum is fast and common in packaging. A multi-finger hand is overkill. Magnetic is for steel parts. A welding torch isn’t for gripping.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quick quiz #2 (EOAT) and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.

slides.explanation_style: Turn Quick quiz #2 (EOAT) into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quick quiz #2 (EOAT) question on their own and explain the reasoning.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slides.narration_seed: Internal sensors help the robot control itself. External sensors help the robot understand the environment.
must_cover: Internal (proprioceptive): joint position, velocity, torque (robot senses itself)
External (exteroceptive): cameras, LiDAR, proximity sensors (robot senses the world)
Internal sensors are required for stable joint control (servo loop)
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
External sensors enable autonomy and flexibility when the environment is not fixed
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Quick quiz #2 (EOAT) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Sensors: internal vs external explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Sensors: internal vs external and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a sensor beam and part into the scene long enough to show what is actually being detected or missed.

slides.narration_seed: If the robot doesn’t know where the joint is, it cannot control accurately. That’s why encoders are everywhere in robotic joints.
must_cover: Encoder measures joint position (angle for revolute, distance for prismatic)
Used for closed-loop control → accuracy and repeatability
Without position feedback, the controller cannot correct error reliably
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Encoders (the #1 sensor in robot joints) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Encoders (the #1 sensor in robot joints) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Define Encoders (the #1 sensor in robot joints) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Encoders (the #1 sensor in robot joints) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a sensor beam and part into the scene long enough to show what is actually being detected or missed.

slide_ref.source_title: How optical encoders measure rotation (basic idea)

slides.narration_seed: Simplest explanation: it’s like counting ticks on a wheel. More ticks means finer resolution and better accuracy.
must_cover: A disk has patterns/tick marks and a sensor reads them as the disk turns
As marks pass, the sensor output changes (pulses or codes)
Incremental: counts pulses → change in position (relative motion)
Absolute: outputs a unique code → true angle at every position
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Incremental vs Absolute encoders so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for How optical encoders measure rotation (basic idea) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture How optical encoders measure rotation (basic idea) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a sensor beam and part into the scene long enough to show what is actually being detected or missed.

slides.narration_seed: Incremental is cheaper but doesn’t know absolute position after power-off. Absolute encoders are common in robot joints because they reduce homing issues.
must_cover: Incremental: measures change in position by counting pulses (relative)
Absolute: provides a position code (true angle) at each instant
Incremental commonly uses A/B signals (quadrature) to detect direction
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Most industrial robots prefer Absolute (position is known after power cycle)
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from How optical encoders measure rotation (basic idea) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Incremental vs Absolute encoders explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Incremental vs Absolute encoders and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a sensor beam and part into the scene long enough to show what is actually being detected or missed.

slides.narration_seed: Correct answer is C. Incremental encoders need a reference after power cycles; absolute encoders store a unique position code.
must_cover: Which encoder type usually needs homing after power-off?
Incremental encoders track change, not absolute position. After power-off, they don’t know the true position, so systems often home to a reference.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quick quiz #3 (encoders) and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quick quiz #3 (encoders) without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quick quiz #3 (encoders) into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quick quiz #3 (encoders) question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: Vision lets robots adapt when the world isn’t perfectly fixed. It’s a big step from pure repeatability to flexible automation.
must_cover: 2D camera: detection, inspection, guidance (where is the part?)
Depth camera: distance + 3D perception (where is it in 3D?)
Common use: pick parts that are not perfectly located or oriented
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into LiDAR / laser scanners (common in mobile robots) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Cameras (vision makes robots flexible) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Cameras (vision makes robots flexible) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Bring a sensor beam and part into the scene long enough to show what is actually being detected or missed.

slide_ref.source_title: LiDAR / laser scanners (common in mobile robots)

slides.narration_seed: In warehouses and hospitals, LiDAR/laser scanners are a big part of autonomy. They help answer: where am I, and what’s in my way?
must_cover: Measures distance by scanning with a laser (range measurements)
Used for mapping (SLAM), localization, and obstacle avoidance
In industry, safety laser scanners can create safety zones around machines
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

slides.narration_seed: If the robot must touch something carefully, force feedback matters. This is key for modern assembly and human-robot collaboration.
must_cover: Measures forces + torques (often 6-axis) at the wrist or tool
Used for insertion, assembly, polishing, collaboration (safe contact)
Enables compliant motion (adjust force) instead of rigid pushing
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quick quiz #4 (sensors) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Force/Torque sensing (for contact tasks) clearly before the formal details continue.

slides.narration_seed: Correct answer is D. Without joint position feedback, the controller cannot correct error accurately.
must_cover: Which sensor is MOST critical for accurate joint control?
Encoders (or resolvers) provide joint position feedback used in the servo control loop. Cameras help perception, but not the basic joint servo loop.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

slide_ref.source_title: Actuators: what makes the robot move?

slides.narration_seed: For industrial robot joints, electric motors dominate. Pneumatics are everywhere in grippers.
must_cover: Actuators generate motion/force at joints (the “muscles” of the robot)
Electric (most common in industrial arms) → precise servo control
Pneumatic (very common for grippers) → fast on/off motion
Hydraulic → very high force for heavy-duty machines
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Actuators: what makes the robot move? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Actuators: what makes the robot move? is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Actuators: what makes the robot move? in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Actuators: what makes the robot move? connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Actuators: what makes the robot move? accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Electric motors are easy to control precisely using encoders. To get high torque, we add gear reduction, and we add brakes for safe holding if power is lost.
must_cover: Uses electric motors + drives (often servo systems with feedback)
Motor drives often use PWM switching to control speed/torque efficiently
Advantages: clean (no fluid leaks), reliable, good for precise positioning
Often uses gearboxes for torque + brakes for safe holding if power is lost
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Actuators: what makes the robot move? into Electric actuation (most common today) so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Types of electric motors (quick reference) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Electric actuation (most common today) to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Electric actuation (most common today) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Electric actuation (most common today) sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: These are the same categories the previous prof highlighted—kept short as a reference.
must_cover: Brushed DC: simple + cheap, but brushes wear (sparks/heat possible)
Brushless DC (BLDC): efficient + low maintenance, needs electronic drive
Stepper: moves in fixed steps; can be open-loop if it doesn’t miss steps
AC motors: robust; speed tied to AC frequency unless using a drive (VFD)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Types of electric motors (quick reference) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Types of electric motors (quick reference) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Types of electric motors (quick reference) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: You asked for an image showing coils/brushes—this one clearly shows the internal parts.
must_cover: Runs on a DC power source (fixed voltage level)
Uses a commutator + brushes to switch current in the rotor coils
Pros: cheap, simple to power/control (basic driver can work)
Cons: brushes wear → maintenance; can create heat/sparking

slide_ref.source_title: Brushless DC (BLDC) vs Stepper (what to remember)

slides.narration_seed: Matches the PDF intent but keeps it short for your deck style.
must_cover: BLDC: DC supply + electronic commutation (drive required) → efficient and durable
BLDC has less wear (no brushes) and typically runs cooler than brushed DC
Stepper: rotates in fixed steps (often ~200 steps/rev in common motors)
Students should identify the main subsystems inside a robot cell.
Stepper can be open-loop if it never misses steps, but torque drops at high speed
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Brushless DC (BLDC) vs Stepper (what to remember) to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into AC motors vs Servo motors (where you’ll see them) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slide_ref.source_title: AC motors vs Servo motors (where you’ll see them)

slides.narration_seed: This keeps the message consistent: a servo is a controlled motor system, not just a motor.
must_cover: AC motors: robust; often fixed speed unless controlled by a VFD/drive
AC motors tend to require less maintenance than brushed DC in many cases
Servo motor/system: motor + sensor (encoder/resolver) + electronics/drive
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Servos are common in robot joints because they support accurate closed-loop control
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Brushless DC (BLDC) vs Stepper (what to remember) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on AC motors vs Servo motors (where you’ll see them) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of AC motors vs Servo motors (where you’ll see them) and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: If you only need open/close or extend/retract, pneumatics are great. But for accurate positioning, electric servo systems are better.
must_cover: Uses pressurized air → cylinders/grippers apply force
Advantages: reliable, inexpensive, simple on/off control
Disadvantages: noisy and needs compressed air supply
Low stiffness (air compresses) → lower accuracy in positioning
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Hydraulics (high force, heavy machines) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Pneumatics (common for simple motions) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Pneumatics (common for simple motions) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Pneumatics (common for simple motions) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Hydraulics are powerful, but messy and maintenance-heavy. That’s why many modern robots prefer electric unless they truly need extreme force.
must_cover: Uses pressurized liquid → very high forces and very high stiffness
Advantages: strong for large robots; can maintain position under heavy loads
High power-to-weight ratio (lots of force for the size)
Disadvantages: leaks/maintenance; susceptible to contamination (dirty fluid)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Hydraulics (high force, heavy machines) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Hydraulics (high force, heavy machines) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.story_hint: Keep Hydraulics (high force, heavy machines) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Hydraulics (high force, heavy machines) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: A motor alone often doesn’t produce enough torque at the joint. Transmission systems match the motor to the real load and motion we want.
must_cover: Motor speed is high; joint speed is lower → transmission matches speed/torque
Gear reduction trades speed for torque (very common in robot joints)
Transmission can transmit motion using belts/chains/cables/pulleys/linkages
Some mechanisms convert motion type: rotation → linear (rack/pinion, ball screw)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Transmission: why we need it to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Transmission: why we need it is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Transmission: why we need it in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Transmission: why we need it connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Transmission: why we need it accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: This is one classic way to convert rotation into linear travel.
must_cover: Motor rotation drives a small gear (pinion)
Pinion teeth push the rack → linear motion
Common when you want strong, simple linear travel from a rotary motor
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Harmonic drive components (exploded view) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Per your request: one harmonic slide focused on components using the exploded view image.
must_cover: Composed of 3 parts: Wave generator, Flex spline, Circular spline
Wave generator: elliptical cam + bearing that creates the deformation
Flex spline: thin flexible cup with external teeth
Circular spline: rigid ring gear with internal teeth

slide_ref.source_title: Harmonic drive motion + pros/cons (why robots use them)

slides.narration_seed: Per your request: second harmonic slide shows motion (GIF) plus advantages/disadvantages.
must_cover: How it works: wave generator deforms the flex spline so teeth engage in two regions
Because tooth counts differ, each wave rotation advances the spline by a small amount
Advantages: high reduction ratio, backlash-free, high accuracy, compact/lightweight, quiet
Disadvantages: torque ripple, fairly elastic (“springy”), nonlinear behavior under load
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

slide_ref.source_title: Ball screw: turning rotation into linear motion

slides.narration_seed: Whenever you see a linear axis moving smoothly and precisely, there’s often a screw mechanism inside converting motor rotation into straight-line motion.
must_cover: Motor rotates a screw; nut travels along the screw → linear travel
Common in linear axes, CNC machines, and precision positioning
High efficiency and good repeatability compared to simple lead screws
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quick quiz #5 (transmissions) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Ball screw: turning rotation into linear motion clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Correct answer is A. ‘Compact + high ratio + low backlash’ is the whole reason harmonic drives are everywhere in wrists.
must_cover: Why do robot wrists often use harmonic drives?
Harmonic drives give high reduction with low backlash in a compact package. Low backlash helps accuracy, especially in wrist orientation.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quick quiz #5 (transmissions) question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: A servo is not just a motor. It’s a motor system that reaches a target because it measures itself and corrects errors continuously.
must_cover: Servo = motor + sensor (encoder/resolver) + drive/controller working together
You command: position / speed / torque (the system closes the loop)
Feedback compares desired vs measured → corrects error many times per second
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Servo motor (the key idea) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slides.story_hint: Keep Servo motor (the key idea) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Servo motor (the key idea) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: PWM is like turning a switch ON/OFF very fast. By changing how long it stays ON, we control average power to the motor.
must_cover: PWM controls average motor voltage/power by switching ON/OFF very fast
Duty cycle = % time ON (higher duty → more average power)
Motor drives use PWM for efficient speed/torque control
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.

slide_ref.source_title: Controller vs Processor vs Software (clear roles)

slides.narration_seed: If you remember one line: The processor calculates what should happen, but the controller makes sure it actually happens using sensor feedback.
must_cover: Software: task logic (sequence, conditions, safety logic → what to do)
Processor: computes motion targets (paths/trajectories → how to move)
Controller: reduces error using feedback so the joint reaches the target
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Actuator/Drive: applies power to produce motion (motor current, valve flow)
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from PWM (simple explanation) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Controller vs Processor vs Software (clear roles) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Controller vs Processor vs Software (clear roles) and know which side fits which situation.

slide_ref.source_title: Feedback loop (why robots can be accurate)

slides.narration_seed: This is the core of robotics control. The robot is constantly correcting itself.
must_cover: Controller compares: desired vs measured (sensor feedback)
Error = desired − measured (difference we want to reduce)
Controller adjusts motor command to reduce error (position/speed/torque)
This repeats many times per second → accuracy despite load/friction changes
must_say: Closed-loop control can correct itself because it measures what actually happened.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Quick quiz #6 (control) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often describe feedback correctly in words but still miss what disturbances do to open-loop behavior.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Feedback loop (why robots can be accurate) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Feedback loop (why robots can be accurate) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Feedback loop (why robots can be accurate) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Correct answer is C. The controller is the ‘error fixer’—it compares desired vs measured and adjusts the motor command until the error becomes small.
must_cover: Which component ensures a joint reaches the correct position?
The actuator produces motion, the processor computes the target, but the controller closes the loop and corrects error to ensure the target is reached.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

slide_ref.source_title: What you will see most often (industry reality)

slides.narration_seed: If you walk into an industrial robotics lab or factory, these are the most common patterns you’ll see. Everything else is a variation or a special case.
must_cover: Joints: mostly Revolute joints in 6-axis arms (compact + large workspace)
End effectors: parallel grippers + vacuum + process tools (weld/paint/dispense)
Sensors: encoders in every joint; cameras added for flexibility
Students should identify the main subsystems inside a robot cell.
Actuation: electric servo joints + pneumatic grippers (common combination)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What you will see most often (industry reality) to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
